Evidence: prior tickets recorded overnight validation and closure after the steps above showed success [Data: Reports (574); Reports (36); Reports (37)]. C. Artifacts you must provide (the indexed records show teams collected these during triage) The indexed corpus shows investigators required specific logs/artifacts to confirm root cause and validate remediation. The records do not contain these artifacts for your environment - you must collect and provide them. (These are recommended evidence‑collection items; the records show the same classes were used in past investigations.) Collect and attach: - CA server event logs and CA audit entries around the weekend maintenance (AIA/CRL publish events and template edits). [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)] - The VPN concentrator / IKE logs that show "client cert chain validation failed" / "EKU mismatch" for the affected user sessions. [Data: Reports (575); Sources (104)] - A representative browser certificate chain screenshot from the HR site showing the chain error ("Issuer's certificate not found"). [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)] - On one or more Domain Controllers: certutil -store MY output and the System/Application event entries showing auto‑enrollment/cert issuance around the weekend (to confirm the DC cert contents and EKUs). [Data: Reports (210); Reports (37)] - Samples of application logs (HR app, SharePoint, NAC) showing LDAPS/TLS handshake failure messages and timestamps. [Data: Reports (210)] - For S/MIME: CA key‑archive audit rows showing recovered keys and PFX export events (if available), and a validated import confirmation from an executive (screenshot or mail test). [Data: Reports (36)] D. What the indexed knowledge does not contain (so I cannot complete the fix from here) - The indexed corpus documents the *process* and prior successful remediations but does not include your tenant/server logs, the CA event log entries, the actual certificate files, or the VPN/IKE traces needed to declare the incident fully closed in your environment. I cannot perform the publish, template reversion, DC re‑enroll or PFX exports for you from the indexed records - those must be executed by your PKI and Identity operators with the artifacts above. [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (36); Reports (575)] E. Immediate recommended actions to start now (these are operational recommendations, marked recommended) - Recommendation: Escalate this ticket to PKI (CA owner), Identity/AD team, and Network/VPN owner immediately and provide the artifact list from Section C. Prior cases were handled by these teams and required cross‑team coordination. [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (575)] - Recommendation: Do not re‑enable any maintenance automation that may reapply templates or reissue certs until you have a controlled runbook (the incidents recommend adding a preflight AIA/CRL verification gate into the runbook). [Data: Reports (574)] - Recommendation: For executives' PFX delivery, use a secure channel and passworded PFX files per the prior S/MIME remediation. [Data: Reports (36)] F. Short, actionable handoff you can copy into the ticket now - Escalation owners to add: PKI owner (on‑call), Identity/AD (owner), Network/VPN owner, and Application owners (HR, SharePoint, NAC). Cite this ticket as "possible PKI / template / AIA publish regression - symptoms: browser CA errors, VPN IKEv2 EKU failures, S/MIME private‑key missing for executives, LDAPS handshake failures." [Data: Reports (574); Reports (575); Reports (36); Reports (210)] - Ask PKI to: (a) confirm whether a CA/issuing/intermediate renewal occurred on the weekend and whether the issuing cert was published to AIA/CRL; (b) if not published, publish the CA cert to AIA and confirm HTTP reachability. (Refer to the prior incident where publishing fixed browser errors.) [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)] - Ask Identity/AD to: (a) confirm whether DC certificate template edits were applied and whether DCs auto‑enrolled incompatible certs; (b) if a template change caused the issue, revert template and force DC re‑enrollment (certutil -pulse) and validate LDAPS. [Data: Reports (210); Reports (37)] - Ask Network/VPN owner to: (a) check concentrator logs for IKE/cert chain/EKU errors; (b) if template lacked IPsec/IKE EKU, reissue VPN server cert from corrected template and re-deploy. [Data: Reports (575); Sources (104)] - Ask PKI to perform executive key recovery and secure PFX delivery for any executives who cannot sign/decrypt; validate import and S/MIME end‑to‑end. [Data: Reports (36)] G. Classification and rationale - Based on the indexed cases with the same multi‑symptom pattern, classify this as second_level_support (requires PKI/Identity/Network engineering escalation). The prior incidents with identical symptoms required engineer‑level template and CA operations, full trust‑chain fixes, and coordinated re‑enrollment and were handled by PKI/Identity owners rather than first‑line support. [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (575); Reports (36)] H. If you want, I will prepare next artifacts for the responders - I can draft the exact log/export request text to send to your CA operator, DC owners, VPN owner and Exchange/PKI team (e.g., exact event logs, certutil outputs, IKE trace snippets and sample sign‑in timestamps). This is a recommended action (not taken verbatim from prior tickets) but will mirror the artifacts used in the recorded remediations. Tell me which team(s) you want me to prepare the request for and I will produce those short templates. Closing / one‑line summary - The indexed evidence maps your four concurrent symptom classes to the documented PKI / certificate‑template / AIA publication failures that occurred in previous weekend maintenance windows. The recorded fix sequence was: publish issuing CA to AIA, revert erroneous template(s) and force DC re‑enrollment, correct VPN template and reissue VPN cert, and recover S/MIME private keys from CA archive and import PFXs - each step is recorded as having restored services in prior incidents [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (575); Reports (36)]. To complete this for your environment we need the artifacts listed in Section C and the PKI/Identity/Network teams to perform the documented remediation steps. If you want those ready‑to‑send artifact‑request messages (one per team), say which teams to include and I will produce them marked as recommended next steps (not as items directly executed in the records).

question_text:
Hi IT, we are a couple of weeks into our migration from our old internal sign-in system to the new cloud-based one. The first batch of users went through smoothly, but since we did the second batch about two weeks ago, we have been getting a series of strange complaints from those users that we cannot make sense of.

First, several users from the second batch say they can sign in to their cloud email and Teams without any problem, but when they try to open our internal procurement system or our document management system (which run on our internal servers), they get an error saying their account is not recognized. The first-batch users do not have this issue.

Second, we are seeing strange inconsistencies with security groups. A user might be correctly in the new combined group on the internal directory side, but in the cloud they still show as a member of an old group. This is breaking some access permissions because the cloud and internal systems read different group memberships for the same person.

Third, the same affected users are saying their mobile email apps (built-in iPhone Mail and the Android Gmail app) keep asking for password and never connect. Outlook on the phone still works fine for them but the built-in mail apps just loop on the password prompt.

Fourth — and this only came up because someone noticed overnight reports were missing — several scheduled tasks that run on our internal servers using service accounts have started failing with authentication errors. The service accounts have not been touched by the migration as far as we know, but the failures started right around the time the second batch was migrated.

These all started in the same window but they look like four completely different problems. We need to figure out what is going on and how to fix it.
answer: Below I separate (A) what the indexed incident records explicitly document about the four symptom classes you reported, and then (B) a concrete, step‑by‑step remediation plan that matches the documented fixes used in prior incidents. Where a step is taken from the recorded incidents I mark it "(documented)" and cite the ticket(s). Where I propose an action that is not present as an executed item in the indexed records I mark it "(recommended)". If something required to reach a full technical conclusion is missing from the indexed records (for example raw AAD Connect sync logs, exported CA policy JSON, Task Scheduler .evtx exports), I call that out and show which artifacts to collect. A. What the indexed records explicitly document (short summary) - UPN / provisioning writeback in wave‑2: - In a prior second‑wave incident investigators found a provisioning/template error that wrote the new cloud UPN suffix back into on‑prem userPrincipalName attributes. That produced exactly the symptom you describe: cloud sign‑in OK, on‑prem apps (that match by UPN) report "user not found" or "account not recognized." Remediation recorded: revert on‑prem UPNs, then run AAD Connect delta + full sync and validate access [documented in TI‑1700001] [Data: Sources (22)]. - OU / AAD Connect sync‑scope omission and group divergence: - Investigations documented cases where groups were created inside a Migration_Phase2 OU that was initially excluded from AAD Connect; cloud continued to show the old group object while on‑prem showed the new group. Remediation recorded: update AAD Connect OU filter, run delta then full sync, and reconcile memberships [documented in TI‑1700002] [Data: Sources (53)]. - Mobile built‑in mail clients looping (EAS / Basic Auth vs Modern Auth): - Prior incidents show built‑in iOS/Android Mail clients using Exchange ActiveSync with Basic Auth were blocked when tenant enforcement required Modern Authentication; Outlook Mobile (OAuth) kept working. Remediation used: force clients to re‑enroll/re‑add to trigger OAuth or use Outlook Mobile / re‑enroll in Intune [documented in KF70 mobile incidents] [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. - Scheduled tasks / service‑accounts failing due to Conditional Access after sync: - Tickets record scheduled‑task failures (Task Scheduler error "Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer.") after service accounts were moved into an OU included by AAD Connect and thus subject to an interactive‑MFA Conditional Access policy. Remediation recorded: correct AAD Connect filter/scope *and* create a dedicated CA exclusion group for non‑interactive service accounts (named KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA in the case record), update the CA policy to exclude it, then validate scheduled tasks. This sequence resolved the scheduled‑task outages in the recorded incident [documented in TI‑1700004] [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. B. Step‑by‑step resolution plan mapped to documented fixes Note: the steps below are ordered and combine (1) immediate triage, (2) identity/sync remediation, (3) CA / service‑account fix, (4) client remediation (mobile), and (5) validation and runbook updates. Each step shows whether it is explicitly documented in the indexed records (and cites those records), or whether it is a recommended next action not itself contained as an executed item in the records. Immediate triage and handoff (do first) 1) Escalate to Identity / AAD Connect engineering (second level). Rationale: similar cases in the indexed corpus required BU Infrastructure - Identity involvement (Employee 30) because the fix touches Azure AD Connect, on‑prem AD attributes, and Conditional Access [documented]. Action: open an engineering handoff and include this case's sample UPN(s), the migration wave timestamp, and the four symptom classes [documented mapping to prior cases] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Sources (49)]. 2) Collect and attach the minimal evidence package for the engineers (recommended - not all artifacts are present in the indexed corpus): sample affected UPNs (1-3), AAD Connect sync run history for the migration window (delta/full job timestamps), current AAD Connect OU filter config (which OUs are IN/OUT), Azure AD Sign‑in log entries for affected UPNs (time window), Task Scheduler event log extracts from servers where scheduled tasks failed (the error lines & timestamps), and a copy/list of Conditional Access policies (or screenshots/JSON export for the policy that looks like the tenant baseline / KF70‑Require‑MFA). Prior incidents used these exact artifacts to diagnose and fix the problem [recommended; the records show these items were requested/used but do not contain the raw exports themselves] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (54); Sources (49); Sources (50)]. Identity / AAD Connect diagnosis and fix 3) Confirm whether on‑prem userPrincipalName attributes were overwritten for the affected users (diagnostic step used in TI‑1700001) - compare on‑prem UPN for sample users to the Entra/Azure AD UPNs and the object's source/immutableId. If the on‑prem UPN was written to the new suffix, revert the on‑prem userPrincipalName to the original value [documented in TI‑1700001]. After reversion run an AAD Connect sync: initial delta to confirm changes then a full sync to ensure propagation. Validate access for the sample users to on‑prem apps [documented remediation] [Data: Sources (22)]. 4) Inspect AAD Connect OU filter / sync scope for the Migration_Phase2 OU and for the OU(s) where service accounts or newly created groups were placed. If Migration_Phase2 or a service‑accounts OU was accidentally excluded or later included incorrectly, adjust the OU filter according to the intended design, then run delta/full syncs (documented pattern). Use Graph/portal membership exports to compare cloud vs on‑prem group objectIDs and membership lists to detect displayName vs ObjectId collisions and to reconcile membership differences [documented diagnostics and remediation used in TI‑1700002 / group sync incidents] [Data: Sources (53); Reports (209); Sources (54)]. Conditional Access / service‑account handling 5) Check whether service accounts are now synchronized to Entra ID (check user "source" attribute). If they are synchronized and you observe scheduled‑task failures with Task Scheduler logon errors at the same timestamps as Azure AD sign‑in log CA failures (e.g., "Failed - Conditional Access requires MFA"), this is exactly the signature recorded in TI‑1700004 [documented]. If so, apply the documented remediation: create a dedicated exclusion group for non‑interactive service accounts (example name used in the record: KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA), add the impacted service accounts to it, and update the Conditional Access policy to exclude that group (so non‑interactive jobs are not forced to do interactive MFA). Validate scheduled tasks run successfully afterward [documented remediation] [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. Notes and caveats: the indexed records show the exclusion group KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA was created and used successfully in TI‑1700004, but they do not contain a tenant export of the full CA policy JSON or a time‑stamped membership CSV for the exclusion group - you must export/capture those artifacts in your tenant as evidence [Data: Sources (50); Entities (755)]. (See "Artifacts you must collect" below.) Mobile client remediation (built‑in mail apps) 6) For the users who see password loops in built‑in Mail apps: the documented pattern shows those clients were attempting legacy Basic Auth (EAS) and being blocked by tenant Modern Authentication enforcement; Outlook Mobile (OAuth) continued to work [documented]. Remediation recorded: on affected devices, remove and re‑add the account as "Microsoft Exchange" (to trigger OAuth) or enroll/re‑enroll devices into Intune / App Protection as required; where the native client cannot be moved to OAuth, use Outlook Mobile as a supported fallback. Validate mailflow on a sample device before advising all users [documented fixes in KF70 mobile incidents] [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. Validation and closure 7) Validate with concrete evidence (documented practice): for sample users confirm (a) cloud sign‑in (successful), (b) on‑prem app sign‑in (successful), (c) the cloud and on‑prem group memberships now match (Graph export diff), (d) mobile client connects properly (OAuth / Outlook Mobile), and (e) scheduled tasks run successfully overnight for affected service accounts. Record the sign‑in log IDs and Task Scheduler event IDs used for validation [these validation steps and evidence types were used in the referenced tickets] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (49); Reports (209)]. 8) Update migration runbook and rollout gates (documented lesson): the recorded incidents updated KF‑MIG‑RB‑v1.4 and added a suffix‑handling requirement and explicit checks for service‑account exclusions, OU filter review, group membership parity and mobile client onboarding for subsequent waves [documented runbook updates after TI‑1700001 / TI‑1700004] [Data: Entities (916); Sources (63); Sources (50)]. Artifacts you must collect (the indexed records do not contain these raw artifacts) - AAD Connect sync run history and the Synchronization Service (miisclient) logs for the relevant date(s) - prior tickets reference running delta/full syncs but the corpus does not contain the raw AAD Connect log files, so get these from your AAD Connect server [records explicitly note the absence of raw engine logs in the indexed set; you must fetch them] [Data: Sources (54); Reports (209)]. - Azure AD sign‑in log entries showing CA evaluation (the records cite sign‑in logs were decisive evidence) - export entries for the sample users and for the service accounts around the failure timestamps [documented approach] [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50)]. - Task Scheduler Operational events (Event Viewer export / .evtx) from the internal servers showing the Task Scheduler error lines (the prior incident cites the message "Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer."). The indexed records show the message but do not embed the raw event file; ask server owners for the exported logs [Data: Sources (49)]. - Conditional Access policy export (policy JSON or screenshots) for the tenant baseline CA (the record describes KF70‑Require‑MFA and the exclusion applied, but does not include a policy JSON export). Export the CA policy to confirm Included and Excluded groups and save the export for audit [Data: Sources (50); Entities (754)]. Who should own each action (documented guidance) - Identity / AAD Connect engineering (BU Infrastructure - Identity) should own AAD Connect OU filter review, UPN reversion, and running delta/full syncs - prior incidents were owned by Employee 30 and similar identity engineers [documented] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (54); Entities (297)]. - Conditional Access / Security team should own the creation/population of the service‑account exclusion group and the CA policy edit (to exclude that group) - TI‑1700004 recorded these edits and owner duties [documented] [Data: Sources (50); Entities (755)]. - Endpoint / Mobile team should own device re‑enrollment and mobile guidance (re‑add as Microsoft Exchange or switch to Outlook Mobile) and Intune validations [documented] [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. When the indexed knowledge is insufficient - The indexed incident corpus documents the root causes and the remediation sequences that resolved the same four symptoms in prior cases (UPN writeback, OU/filter issues, CA applying to service accounts, EAS/Basic Auth mobile loops). However, the corpus does NOT contain the raw AAD Connect sync logs, the tenant CA policy JSON exports, nor the server Task Scheduler .evtx files needed to prove precisely which objects changed and at what timestamps in your environment. Those artifacts must be collected from your tenant and servers to complete the investigation end‑to‑end (and prior tickets explicitly requested and used them during diagnosis) [Data: Sources (54); Sources (50); Sources (49)]. Concise suggested immediate message to include in the handoff to Identity (documented handoff elements) - Short escalation note (use when you open the engineer ticket): "Second migration wave (week of ~06.01.25) produced: (1) cloud sign‑in OK but on‑prem apps failing (probable on‑prem UPN suffix overwrite); (2) cloud vs on‑prem group membership divergence (possible Migration_Phase2 OU / AAD Connect OU‑filter omission); (3) built‑in mobile mail clients looping (EAS/Basic Auth vs Modern Auth enforcement); (4) scheduled tasks failing for service accounts (service accounts may have been synced and are now evaluated by baseline CA). Please investigate AAD Connect OU filter, on‑prem UPN values, AAD Connect sync logs, Azure AD sign‑in logs (sample UPNs attached), and Task Scheduler event logs. Consider creating/using a dedicated exclusion group for non‑interactive service accounts if CA is blocking scheduled jobs (example used in prior case: KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA)." (This is a recommended handoff text built from the documented remediation actions and owner decisions in TI‑1700001 / TI‑1700002 / TI‑1700004) [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. If you want I can: - Produce a short checklist of the exact export fields to ask your AAD Connect admin and the server owners for (AAD Connect run logs, Sync Service report, Entra sign‑in log slice, Task Scheduler .evtx event export, CA policy export). (This is a recommended artifact to gather - prior incidents relied on precisely these exports but the exports themselves are not present in the indexed records.) - Or, if you prefer, I can draft the exact handoff ticket text/email to Employee 30 / BU Infrastructure - Identity including the ticket references (TI‑1700001, TI‑1700002, TI‑1700004) so the engineer has context from the historical cases cited above [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Sources (49)]. References (key indexed records I used) - TI‑1700001 - second‑wave UPN suffix overwrite, revert on‑prem UPNs, run full AAD Connect sync, validate on‑prem app access [Data: Sources (22)]. - TI‑1700002 - Migration_Phase2 OU / AAD Connect OU filter omission, update OU filter, run delta + full sync, reconcile group membership [Data: Sources (53)]. - TI‑1700004 - scheduled‑task outages: service accounts synchronized to Entra, CA requirement of interactive MFA blocked non‑interactive logons; remediation: correct AAD Connect scope and create CA exclusion group KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA; validate tasks [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. - KF‑MIG‑RB‑v1.4 and migration runbook updates - per‑wave validation checklist items (cloud sign‑in, on‑prem app sign‑in, mobile mail, scheduled tasks/service accounts) and suffix‑handling requirement added after incidents [Data: Entities (916); Sources (63)]. - Mobile/EAS cases - built‑in iOS/Android Mail clients using EAS/Basic Auth blocked by Modern Auth enforcement; Outlook Mobile/OAuth worked; remediation via re‑add / Intune re‑enroll or app switch documented in KF70 mobile incidents [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. Bottom line - The four different‑looking symptoms in your ticket map to a known single class of hybrid‑identity migration failures documented in the indexed records: (a) provisioning/template UPN writeback, (b) AAD Connect OU/sync scope errors causing group/object divergence, (c) legacy Basic‑Auth mobile clients blocked by Modern Auth enforcement, and (d) service accounts becoming subject to interactive MFA via CA after being synchronized.
